title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: Six paragraphs have been removed from the text. Choose the correct paragraph (A-G) to fill each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.title: Nature’s R&D: The Rise of Biomimicry
content.text: For billions of years, nature has been conducting a vast, unplanned experiment in engineering. Through the process of natural selection, evolution has solved complex problems of energy efficiency, structural integrity, and self-repair. Biomimicry is the practice of looking to these biological blueprints to solve human challenges. || (41) || [41] One of the most famous examples of this approach occurred in the 1940s [/41]. After a walk in the woods, Swiss engineer George de Mestral noticed how burrs from plants clung to his dog’s fur. This simple observation led to the invention of Velcro, a product that revolutionized fastening systems worldwide. || (42) || [42] Today, the scale of such research has moved from simple mechanical fasteners to complex aerodynamic structures [/42]. Engineers in Japan, for instance, looked to the kingfisher to solve a problem with their high-speed 'bullet' trains. The train's blunt nose was creating a deafening sonic boom when exiting tunnels. || (43) || [43] By mimicking this shape, the train not only became quieter but also 15% more energy-efficient [/43]. This success story highlights how biological forms can lead to superior mechanical performance. However, biomimicry isn't just about copying shapes; it's about understanding the underlying chemistry. || (44) || [44] For researchers, the holy grail is replicating the way a spider produces silk [/44]. Pound for pound, spider silk is stronger than steel and tougher than Kevlar, yet it is produced at room temperature using only water as a solvent. If we could manufacture materials this way, we could eliminate the toxic, high-heat processes common in modern factories. || (45) || [45] Moving beyond individual materials, some architects are now looking at entire ecosystems [/45]. In Zimbabwe, the Eastgate Centre office complex uses a cooling system inspired by the self-ventilating mounds of African termites. The building stays at a comfortable temperature without any conventional air conditioning. || (46) || [46] Despite these triumphs, the field faces significant hurdles [/46]. Nature builds with a limited palette of abundant elements, while human industry relies on rare and often toxic minerals. Bridging the gap between biological growth and industrial manufacturing remains the greatest challenge for the next generation of designers.

paragraphs.A: This transition to more sustainable production methods is often called 'the second industrial revolution.' By adopting nature's manufacturing techniques, we could create products that are fully biodegradable and carbon-neutral.
paragraphs.B: [43] They realized that the bird’s long, wedge-shaped beak allows it to dive from air into water with hardly a splash [/43]. The beak's geometry reduces the change in pressure, a principle that could be applied to a train moving between the open air and a tunnel.
paragraphs.C: [41] It is a discipline that treats the natural world not as a source of raw materials, but as a mentor [/41]. By studying the 'riddles' of survival, scientists are finding that nature has already done the heavy lifting of research and development.
paragraphs.D: [45] These structures maintain a constant internal climate despite external temperatures fluctuating by over 30 degrees [/45]. This is achieved through a complex network of chimneys that open and close to regulate airflow, a feat of 'passive' engineering that humans are only now beginning to master.
paragraphs.E: [44] Biological organisms are experts at low-energy manufacturing [/44]. While we use 'heat, beat, and treat' methods—extreme temperatures and high pressure—to create materials, nature assembles complex molecules at ambient temperatures.
paragraphs.F: The term 'biomimicry' was first popularized in the 1990s, but the concept has roots in the work of Leonardo da Vinci, who studied the flight of birds to design his flying machines. This long history shows our enduring fascination with natural mechanics.
paragraphs.G: [42] It was a classic case of finding a high-tech solution in a low-tech environment [/42]. De Mestral’s curiosity turned a minor annoyance into a multi-million dollar industry, proving that inspiration is often hiding in plain sight.

questions.explanation: C define la disciplina como 'mentor' y enlaza con el texto principal que menciona los 'biomimicry' blueprints.
G comenta el éxito de De Mestral (Velcro), conectando su 'minor annoyance' con la invención revolucionaria mencionada.
B explica la forma del pico del martín pescador (kingfisher), que es la solución al problema de los túneles planteado.
E introduce el concepto de 'low-energy manufacturing' de la naturaleza, que precede al ejemplo de la seda de araña.
D describe el funcionamiento de los termiteros, explicando cómo mantienen el clima antes de mencionar el edificio de Zimbabue.
A habla de la transición a métodos sostenibles, lo cual conecta con el cierre del texto sobre el reto de la industria frente a la naturaleza.
